reflection.quote: We are what we repeatedly do. Excellence, then, is not an act, but a habit.
reflection.reading:
It's a Wednesday in late May. Nothing is happening. The week isn't won or lost yet. No one is throwing you a party for not drinking on a Wednesday — and that's exactly the point.

When I was drinking, I lived for the weekends and white-knuckled the days in between. Tuesday was an obstacle. Wednesday was a dare. I thought a real life was the loud moments — the festivals, the trips, the nights that turned into mornings. What I missed was that the loud moments were maybe five percent of my life, and the other ninety-five was me waiting, hungover, half-present, planning the next escape from a life I'd built specifically to escape from. Sobriety didn't take the loud moments away. It gave me the ninety-five back.

A quiet Wednesday in May is not a consolation prize. It's the actual prize. Coffee that tastes like coffee. A walk where you notice the trees have leafed out. A conversation with someone you love where you remember every word of it tomorrow. The version of you that gets to live this day fully — that person is built on Wednesdays. Not on the dramatic decisions, but on the ordinary ones, made again and again, until the life you couldn't picture starts picturing itself around you.
reflection.meditation: What ordinary moment today am I actually awake for?

affirmations: I am building my life on the quiet days, not just the loud ones.
Today I get to be fully present for whatever this Wednesday brings.
I am someone who shows up — for myself, for the people I love, for the small things that add up.

gratitude.prayer: Today I'm grateful for a regular Wednesday — nothing on fire, nothing to escape from. I'm grateful for the coffee I actually tasted this morning, and for a clear head to do the work in front of me. There was a time I couldn't imagine wanting a day like this. Now I know it's the whole point.
gratitude.closing: One ordinary day at a time.
